label: Guides
title: Everything you need to know about living here.
subtitle: From day one to the residency application process—clear, practical guides written by people who’ve been through it.
help_title: Can't find what you're looking for?
help_desc: The community has answers to almost any question about living in Uruguay.

primeras48.subtitle: You're here. Take a deep breath. This checklist covers everything you need to do—in order of priority, with helpful links and numbers.
primeras48.stat_label: Things to do when you arrive
primeras48.footer_title: Would you like the complete guide in PDF format?
primeras48.footer_desc: Download it for free — it works offline.

steps.title: Buy a SIM card
steps.subtitle: Antel or Claro — at the same airport
steps.body: There are Antel and Claro stores in the arrivals area. A 30-day plan with unlimited data costs between $400–600 UYU (~$10–15 USD).
tips: I signed up for a data plan, not just voice service
Bring cash—they don't always accept cards

steps.title: Exchange money
steps.subtitle: Currency exchange in Carrasco
steps.body: There's a currency exchange booth at the airport. The exchange rate isn't the best, but exchange enough for the taxi and your first few days (~$100 USD).
tips: Airport rates are 5–8% higher than in the city
Bringing US dollars is the best option when you arrive
ATMs dispense pesos — look for "Scotiabank" or "BROU"

steps.title: Transportation to downtown
steps.subtitle: Taxi, Uber, or bus
steps.body: Uber works perfectly from Carrasco. An official taxi costs about $1,500 UYU (~$37 USD) to downtown/Pocitos. Bus 700 goes to Ciudad Vieja for about $50 UYU but takes 1 hour and 30 minutes.
steps.infoBody: Uber is reliable, cheaper than a taxi, and the driver already knows where you're going. Download the app before you land.

steps.title: Download Prex
steps.subtitle: Uruguayan digital wallet
steps.body: Prex is the most widely used digital wallet in Uruguay. You can load it with cash at any RedPagos location and use it to pay at stores, for delivery, and for services.
tips: Download the app and sign up using your passport
Top it up at any RedPagos or Abitab location
It works for paying for Uber, Pedidos Ya, etc.

steps.title: Install the essential apps
steps.subtitle: Taxis, delivery, maps
steps.body: The apps you'll use every day in Uruguay:
tips: Google Maps — works perfectly in Montevideo
Urufarma — Find pharmacies and medication prices

steps.title: Get to know your neighborhood
steps.subtitle: Supermarket, pharmacy, ATM
steps.body: The first things you'll want to find near your accommodation are a supermarket (Tienda Inglesa, Disco, Ta-Ta), a pharmacy, an ATM, and a bakery.
steps.infoBody: Tienda Inglesa (premium), Disco/Devoto (mid-range), Ta-Ta (budget). All of them carry good products and accept credit cards.

steps.title: Identity card
steps.subtitle: Uruguayan email address or DNIC
steps.body: If you plan to stay for more than 90 days, start the process of obtaining an ID card. You’ll need your passport and a proof of address. You can apply at Correo Uruguayo, and it takes 2–4 weeks.
tips: Bring your original passport and a photocopy
You need a Uruguayan address (it can be a temporary residence)

steps.title: Mutualista said
steps.subtitle: Healthcare system
steps.body: Uruguay has a public-private healthcare system. If you are an employee, DISSE assigns you a health insurance provider. If you are a freelancer or remote worker, you can sign up for one directly.
steps.infoBody: You can sign up for private health insurance (Médica Uruguaya, CASMU, etc.) for about $80–120 USD per month. It covers doctors' visits, emergency care, and basic tests.

steps.title: Join NomadUY
steps.subtitle: The community that makes all of this easier
steps.body: The NomadUY community has WhatsApp groups organized by topic (housing, work, legal, social), a Q&A forum, and monthly events in Montevideo.
tips: Monthly event: the first Thursday of every month
